draft.introduction: At Philebus 20c–22b, Socrates examines what constitutes the good life: is it pleasure, knowledge, or some mixture of both? He argues that neither pleasure without wisdom nor wisdom without pleasure is self-sufficient or choiceworthy.

translation.text: The good must be perfect and sufficient. All who can pursue it do pursue it, and everyone wishes to seize it and make it his own, caring for nothing else except what is connected to the possession of goods. Consider now: would you choose to live your whole life enjoying the greatest pleasures? If you would, you would need no further share of intelligence or memory or right opinion.
translation.original_text: τοῦτο τοίνυν τὸ ἀγαθὸν τέλεον εἶναι δεῖ καὶ ἱκανόν

translator_notes.note: The argument that the good must be “perfect and sufficient” (τέλεον καὶ ἱκανόν) became the formal definition of happiness in Aristotle’s Nicomachean Ethics. The mixed life combining pleasure and wisdom anticipates the Aristotelian and Stoic emphasis on comprehensive human flourishing.
